title: FPN Architecture
concept: FPN combines bottom-up feature extraction with a top-down upsampling pathway, linked by lateral 1x1 convolutions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main limitation of early CNN layers for object detection?

options.text: They contain fine details but lack the semantic context to classify objects
They are too slow to calculate on GPU

exercises.explain: Correct! Early layers capture raw edges and textures but lack the high-level semantic meaning needed for classification.

cards.title: The Feature Bridge
cards.body: How can we build a network that combines the big-picture view with fine spatial details? A Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) does this by building a bridge between early and deep layers. Let's look at its architecture.

cards.title: FPN Architecture
cards.body: A Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) consists of three components: a bottom-up pathway (standard CNN backbone), a top-down pathway (upsampling semantic features), and lateral connections that merge them at every resolution scale.

cards.title: The Double-Sided Ladder
cards.body: Think of FPN like a double-sided ladder. One side goes up (bottom-up: extracting features and shrinking size). The other side goes down (top-down: upsampling semantics). Horizontal rungs (lateral connections) connect the two sides to keep features aligned.

cards.title: Lateral Merging
cards.body: FPN takes ResNet's deep 7x7 feature map. It upsamples it to 14x14 to match an earlier layer. It passes the earlier 14x14 layer through a 1x1 convolution (lateral connection) to align channels, and merges them using element-wise addition.

cards.title: Simple Pixel Copying?
cards.body: You might think lateral connections just copy raw pixels. Fact: Lateral connections use 1x1 convolutions to perform dimension reduction on early features, matching channel depth so they can be merged cleanly with upsampled features.

left.text:
Backbone extracts spatial features
Lateral 1x1 conv adjusts channels
Preserves clean coordinates

right.text:
Upsamples deep semantic maps
Adds them to lateral features
Outputs multi-scale pyramid

exercises.prompt: What are the three core pathways in a Feature Pyramid Network?

options.text: Bottom-up pathway, top-down pathway, and lateral connections
Input pathway, activation pathway, and dropout connections
Forward pathway, backward pathway, and pooling connections
Convolution pathway, batchnorm pathway, and residual connections

exercises.explain: Correct! FPN uses bottom-up, top-down, and lateral connections to form the pyramid.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Lateral connections in FPN use 1x1 convolutions to match the channel dimensions of the bottom-up and top-down feature maps.
exercises.explain: True. 1x1 convolutions align the channel sizes so the feature maps can be added together element-wise.

exercises.sentence: FPN merges high-level semantics with low-level details by ___ deep feature maps to match earlier sizes.
exercises.explain: Upsampling (or bilinear interpolation) increases the resolution of deep features to match early features.

exercises.prompt: What is the function of the top-down pathway in FPN?

options.text: To pass high-level semantic information down to higher-resolution layers
To scale down the input image resolution
To classify the background pixels of the image

exercises.explain: Correct! The top-down pathway upsamples deep semantic features to share context with earlier, detailed layers.

exercises.prompt: You are looking at an FPN diagram. You notice that features from ResNet are passed through a 1x1 convolution before being added to the upsampled top-down features. Why is this 1x1 convolution necessary?

options.text: It reduces the number of channels of the ResNet feature map to match the channel count of the upsampled features exactly.
It performs max pooling to shrink the image.
It changes the image from RGB to grayscale.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The 1x1 convolution acts as a channel bridge, ensuring different layers have compatible dimensions before merging. Phase 8: Modern Object Detection is complete. Next up: Image Segmentation.
